content.text: ElizaOS v2.0.0 was officially released on May 19, 2026, announced in the community discussion channel and met with positive reactions. Community moderator Odilitime confirmed the release has a heavy focus on native apps. Several community members returning after time away or joining for the first time asked about the current state of the platform, how to use it, and whether video or live examples exist. An upcoming Agent Dev Talk Twitter Space hosted by Odilitime and cozydev was shared as a live audio venue for developers to discuss agents. A community member also noted that v3 is expected to launch soon.

Shaw posted an image showcasing Eliza v3 running on ElizaOS (Linux) on an emulated RISC-V chip referred to as the Eliza E1. Community member Zaday celebrated this achievement, describing it as ElizaOS Debian RISC-V 64-bit ISO successfully booting on the QEMU chip emulator in 35 seconds. This sparked curiosity about whether the team intends to develop their own dedicated chip for ElizaOS in the future.

waifu.fun, an open source agentic launchpad built by shad0w and Sol and powered by elizaOS v3 agents, officially launched. The platform allows tokenized agents to launch and run on Eliza Cloud, with their applications generating revenue that can be shared with holders. The first agent on the platform is Sol, The Architect, who reportedly built waifu.fun itself. The source code is publicly available on GitHub at github.com/waifufun/waifu.fun. The announcement was made by Shaw to the entire server community. A follow-up discussion on May 24 confirmed that Sol the Architect and an Eliza agent built the majority of this launchpad.

Core team member Odilitime clarified several token economics questions. Milady and elizaOS are not the same legal entity, as the team took fees and built something on their own stack. Milady has its own cloud infrastructure, and profits from it would fuel Milady buybacks rather than elizaOS buybacks, with the BNB version of the Milady token receiving those buybacks. No additional tokens beyond the existing ones are planned, with a possible Babylon token considered far in the future. There is currently no formalized buyback percentage, as the focus remains on achieving profitability first. Shaw confirmed that the most liquidity for ElizaOS tokens is currently on BSC, and noted that the ai16z to ElizaOS migration is no longer available as the team that managed that process has moved on.

Shaw clarified that the previously mentioned Babylon airdrop to ElizaOS holders did not happen due to running out of funds, and confirmed the team is now fully focused on ElizaOS. Zaday expressed strong support for this direction, advocating that the core team concentrate solely on the ElizaOS token and build multiple utility use cases for it similar to ETH or SOL. A community member asking about the Babylon project after being unable to log in was told by Shaw that the project ran out of funding and was shut down, with the team having moved on. Some elements of Babylon, specifically training simulations, have been repurposed and integrated into the new ElizaOS under the name Feed, though the full game was not carried over.

In the coders channel, several developers introduced themselves and shared projects throughout the week. _0rkz_ introduced BYTE Protocol, a per-byte data settlement layer built on Arbitrum where publishers stake tokens and broadcast data feeds while agents pay per kilobyte, with over 10,000 messages settled on testnet and 8 live feeds including BTC price and a fact-oracle backed by Claude web search. JanaBr announced an Eliza plugin for DeFi agents on Base and Aave, published to NPM under centinel-risk-protocol/eliza-plugin-centinel, offering real-time solvency scoring and flash-crash simulations. A VoiceAI white label agency founder discussed challenges of dockerizing their platform, noting progress reducing their system to two containers and working toward a single container solution. A developer offered advice on separating services such as inference, API, database, and queue or worker, then containerizing them gradually. A founder also asked how AI agent teams handle security when agents have wallet access, covering spending caps and prompt injection.

Throughout the week, numerous developers introduced themselves across the general and coders channels. Backgrounds represented included full-stack AI systems, LLM orchestration, RAG pipelines, multimodal AI, automation, smart contracts, dApp integrations, and SaaS platforms. Technologies mentioned included React, Node.js, Python, OpenAI API, PyTorch, Next.js, TypeScript, Prisma, PostgreSQL, Redis, Docker, Kubernetes on AWS, LangGraph, CrewAI, Claude 3.5, GPT-4o, Pinecone, and Weaviate. Many expressed openness to freelance or collaborative opportunities and interest in connecting with founders working on long-term projects.

Development on May 18, 2026 centered on cross-platform stability and foundational infrastructure for deployment and benchmarking. Windows fallbacks were implemented for plugin-lifeops and circular symlink issues in the Docker pruner were resolved. CI/CD capabilities were expanded with experimental ARM64 support and Flatpak manifests prepared for offline-only build environments. The foundation for the elizaos deploy CLI was launched to streamline monetized app management. Additionally, a side-by-side comparison harness for SWE-bench Lite was integrated to enable performance evaluation against opencode.
